sections.heading: What is a Fragment?
sections.content: A Fragment is a reusable, self-contained portion of a UI that lives inside an Activity. Think of a Fragment as a modular sub-screen — it has its own layout, its own lifecycle, and its own logic, but it always requires a host Activity to exist. Fragments were introduced to allow Android apps to build flexible, multi-pane UIs that adapt to different screen sizes — for example, showing a list and detail panel side by side on a tablet, but as separate screens on a phone.

sections.heading: Why Use Fragments?
items: Modularity — break complex screens into smaller, focused, reusable UI components
Adaptability — show multiple Fragments side by side on tablets, stacked on phones
Navigation — Jetpack Navigation Component uses Fragments as navigation destinations
Back stack management — FragmentManager maintains its own back stack for Fragment transactions
Shared ViewModel — Fragments within the same Activity can share a ViewModel for communication
Lifecycle awareness — Fragments have their own lifecycle separate from but tied to their host Activity
Dialog Fragments — show dialogs as Fragments for proper lifecycle and state management
Bottom navigation — each tab in a bottom navigation bar is typically backed by a Fragment

rows.activity: A full standalone screen managed by the Android OS
rows.fragment: A modular UI component hosted inside an Activity

rows.activity: Managed directly by the Android system
rows.fragment: Tied to the host Activity lifecycle with additional Fragment-specific callbacks
Multiple Fragments can coexist on one screen
Via shared ViewModel, Fragment Result API, or callbacks

rows.activity: Use one Activity as host (Single Activity Architecture)
rows.fragment: Use Fragments for all screens with Navigation Component

sections.heading: The Fragment Lifecycle
sections.content: The Fragment lifecycle is more complex than the Activity lifecycle because a Fragment has two distinct lifecycles: the Fragment lifecycle itself and the separate View lifecycle. The Fragment object can exist without its View — for example, when placed on the back stack, the Fragment's View is destroyed but the Fragment instance remains alive. This distinction is critical for correct resource management, especially when observing LiveData or collecting Flows.

rows.called_when: Fragment is attached to its host Activity
rows.typical_use: Get reference to host Activity or context; verify host implements required interface

rows.called_when: Fragment is being created (View not yet created)
rows.typical_use: Initialize non-UI components: ViewModel, arguments, retained data

rows.called_when: Fragment creates its UI View hierarchy
rows.typical_use: Inflate and return the Fragment's layout using the inflater

rows.called_when: View is fully created and ready
rows.typical_use: Set up UI: click listeners, RecyclerView, observe LiveData, collect Flow

rows.called_when: Saved state has been restored into the View hierarchy
rows.typical_use: Restore any custom View state not handled automatically

rows.called_when: Fragment becomes visible to the user
rows.typical_use: Same as Activity onStart — register visible-state listeners

rows.called_when: Fragment is interactive and in the foreground
rows.typical_use: Resume camera, sensors, or media playback

rows.called_when: Fragment is losing focus but may still be visible
rows.typical_use: Pause playback, sensors, or ongoing animations
Stop background work tied to visibility

rows.called_when: System saves Fragment state before destruction
rows.typical_use: Save lightweight UI state to the Bundle for restoration

rows.called_when: Fragment's View is being destroyed (Fragment may still exist)
rows.typical_use: Clear View Binding reference to prevent memory leaks; stop View-related work
Release all remaining resources not tied to the View

rows.called_when: Fragment is detached from its host Activity
rows.typical_use: Final cleanup; null out any Activity references

sections.heading: Fragment Lifecycle vs View Lifecycle
items: The Fragment object lifecycle: onAttach → onCreate → onCreateView → onViewCreated → ... → onDestroyView → onDestroy → onDetach
The Fragment's View lifecycle is shorter: created in onCreateView, destroyed in onDestroyView
viewLifecycleOwner is tied to the View lifecycle — use it (not 'this') for observing LiveData in Fragments
Using 'this' (Fragment) as LifecycleOwner for LiveData can cause multiple observer registrations after back stack pop
View Binding references must be nulled out in onDestroyView() to avoid memory leaks
The Fragment instance survives back stack — its View does not; onCreateView is called again on return
ViewModel survives both View and Fragment destruction — it lives as long as the host Activity

sections.heading: Fragment Lifecycle vs Activity Lifecycle Comparison

rows.activity: N/A (Activity has no separate view lifecycle)

sections.heading: Adding Fragments to an Activity
sections.content: There are two ways to add a Fragment to an Activity: statically in XML using the <fragment> tag (or <androidx.fragment.app.FragmentContainerView>), or dynamically at runtime using a FragmentTransaction. The static approach is simpler but inflexible. The dynamic approach using FragmentManager and FragmentTransaction is the standard modern pattern — it lets you add, replace, remove, and animate Fragments at runtime.

sections.heading: Dynamic Fragment Transactions
items: Get the FragmentManager: supportFragmentManager (in Activity) or parentFragmentManager / childFragmentManager (in Fragment)
Begin a transaction: supportFragmentManager.beginTransaction()
add(containerId, fragment) — adds a Fragment to the container; existing Fragment stays in memory
replace(containerId, fragment) — replaces the current Fragment in the container with a new one
remove(fragment) — removes the Fragment from the container
hide(fragment) / show(fragment) — hides or shows a Fragment without destroying it
addToBackStack(name) — adds this transaction to the back stack so the user can press Back to reverse it
commit() — schedules the transaction asynchronously; use commitNow() for immediate synchronous execution
setCustomAnimations(enter, exit, popEnter, popExit) — adds enter/exit animations to the transition

sections.heading: add() vs replace() in Fragment Transactions

rows.add: Adds the new Fragment on top of any existing Fragment in the container
rows.replace: Removes the current Fragment and adds the new one in its place

rows.add: Stays alive and visible beneath (if not covered)
rows.replace: Destroyed (onDestroyView called) unless added to back stack

rows.add: Popping reverses the add — the new Fragment is removed
rows.replace: Popping re-creates the previous Fragment's View

rows.add: Both Fragments stay in memory simultaneously
rows.replace: Previous Fragment's View is destroyed — more memory efficient

rows.add: Overlaying Fragments (dialog-like), tab switching with hide/show
rows.replace: Standard screen navigation — the most common pattern

sections.heading: Passing Data to Fragments — Arguments Bundle
items: Never pass data to a Fragment via constructor parameters — the system recreates Fragments using a no-arg constructor
Use a Bundle arguments: val fragment = MyFragment().apply { arguments = bundleOf("userId" to 42) }
bundleOf() is a Kotlin extension from androidx.core.os that creates a Bundle concisely
Read arguments inside the Fragment: val userId = arguments?.getInt("userId") ?: 0
Define argument keys as constants in a companion object to avoid typos
Use a static factory method (newInstance) pattern to encapsulate argument creation
For type-safe argument passing, use the Jetpack Navigation Component's Safe Args plugin
Arguments survive configuration changes and process death — they are part of the Fragment's saved state

rows.description: Both Fragments get the same ViewModel scoped to the host Activity using activityViewModels()
rows.best_for: Sibling Fragments that need to share or react to the same data

rows.description: One Fragment sets a result with setFragmentResult(); another listens with setFragmentResultListener()
rows.best_for: One-time results from a child Fragment back to its parent (e.g., dialog selection)

rows.description: Fragment defines an interface; host Activity implements it; Fragment calls it via onAttach()
rows.best_for: Legacy pattern — replaced by ViewModel and Result API in modern code

rows.description: Pass typed arguments between Fragment destinations via the Navigation Component
rows.best_for: Passing data during screen navigation with type safety

rows.description: Fragment holds a direct reference to another Fragment or Activity
rows.best_for: Never — causes tight coupling and potential memory leaks

sections.heading: Fragment Result API
items: The Fragment Result API is the modern, lifecycle-safe way to send results between Fragments
The sender calls: setFragmentResult("requestKey", bundleOf("result" to value))
The receiver registers a listener in onViewCreated(): setFragmentResultListener("requestKey") { key, bundle -> }
The listener is automatically removed when the Fragment's View is destroyed — no manual cleanup needed
Results are delivered only when the recipient Fragment is at least in the STARTED state
If a result is set before the listener is registered, it is held and delivered once the listener is ready
Use it for dialog results, bottom sheet confirmations, or any child-to-parent Fragment communication

rows.dialog_fragment: Fully lifecycle-aware — survives rotation without crashing
rows.alert_dialog: Not lifecycle-aware — dismissed on rotation, can cause window leaks

rows.dialog_fragment: Automatically saves and restores dialog state
rows.alert_dialog: Must be recreated manually after configuration change

rows.dialog_fragment: Use Fragment Result API for clean decoupled results
rows.alert_dialog: Requires direct callback reference — can cause memory leaks

rows.dialog_fragment: Can be added to Fragment back stack
Always preferred for dialogs in modern Android
rows.alert_dialog: Only for very simple, transient dialogs with no state concerns

sections.heading: ViewBinding in Fragments — Avoiding Memory Leaks
items: In Fragments, View Binding requires special handling because the View lifecycle is shorter than the Fragment lifecycle
Declare binding as nullable: private var _binding: FragmentHomeBinding? = null
Create a non-null accessor: private val binding get() = _binding!!
Inflate in onCreateView(): _binding = FragmentHomeBinding.inflate(inflater, container, false); return binding.root
Always null out the binding in onDestroyView(): _binding = null
Failing to null the binding in onDestroyView() causes a memory leak — the binding holds View references that should be released
Never access binding after onDestroyView() — the Views no longer exist and the reference is null

rows.manages: Fragments directly hosted in the Activity's layout
The FragmentManager of the Fragment's direct parent (Activity or parent Fragment)
Fragments nested inside this Fragment's own layout

sections.heading: Best Practices for Fragments
items: Adopt Single Activity Architecture — one host Activity with all screens as Fragments managed by Navigation Component
Always use viewLifecycleOwner when observing LiveData or collecting Flow inside a Fragment
Null out View Binding in onDestroyView() every time — this is a mandatory memory leak prevention step
Use the Fragment Result API instead of interface callbacks for Fragment-to-Fragment communication
Pass data via arguments Bundle or Safe Args — never via constructor parameters
Scope ViewModels to the Activity (activityViewModels()) when data must be shared between sibling Fragments
Prefer replace() over add() for navigation-style Fragment transactions to avoid stacking invisible Fragments
Use FragmentContainerView instead of FrameLayout as the Fragment host container in your Activity layout

rows.fix: Use arguments Bundle or Safe Args — system recreates Fragments with a no-arg constructor

rows.mistake: Using 'this' as LifecycleOwner for LiveData observation
rows.fix: Use viewLifecycleOwner — prevents stale observers accumulating after back stack returns
Always set _binding = null in onDestroyView() to prevent View memory leaks

rows.mistake: Doing UI work in onCreate() instead of onViewCreated()
rows.fix: Access Views only in onViewCreated() or later — Views don't exist in onCreate()
Use requireActivity() if you're sure Fragment is attached, or check isAdded() first
Use commitAllowingStateLoss() if the transaction can safely be lost after state save

sections.heading: Summary
sections.content: Fragments are the modular building blocks of modern Android UI. Their dual lifecycle — Fragment lifecycle and View lifecycle — requires careful handling, especially around View Binding, LiveData observation, and data passing. Adopt the Single Activity Architecture with Jetpack Navigation Component, always use viewLifecycleOwner for UI observations, communicate via shared ViewModels and the Fragment Result API, and you will build Fragment-based apps that are robust, memory-efficient, and easy to maintain.
